content.text: When multiple teams are building toward a shared launch, individual sprint progress tells only part of the story. What matters to the program manager is whether the integration points between teams are moving toward readiness on schedule. An integration milestone is a cross-team checkpoint — a moment where Team A's output must meet Team B's requirements before combined work can proceed. Tracking these milestones separately from internal team tasks gives the program a shared language for readiness.

attrs.alt: Swimlane chart showing integration milestones across Platform, Data Engineering, Product, and Security teams

content.text: The distinction matters for meeting design, escalation paths, and how status is reported:
Two or more teams with a handoff

Each integration milestone captures its gate dependencies — the preceding milestones that must be achieved before it can start. This models the real constraint: Product Teams cannot begin integration builds until both the API (M1) and RAG index (M2) are ready.
The plan structure links milestones through gate relationships, making the dependency chain explicit and machine-checkable:

from datetime import date

plan = IntegrationPlan(program="Claude Enterprise Q3")

plan.milestones = [
    IntegrationMilestone(
        "M1", "API Ready", "Claude API provisioned + security hardened",
        owning_team="Platform", target_date=date(2025, 8, 28),
        gates=[],
    ),
    IntegrationMilestone(
        "M2", "RAG Ready", "RAG index built and validated",
        owning_team="Data Engineering", target_date=date(2025, 9, 4),
        gates=[],
    ),
    IntegrationMilestone(
        "M3", "Integration Test", "All product team integrations passing",
        owning_team="Product Teams", target_date=date(2025, 9, 18),
        gates=["M1", "M2"],
    ),
    IntegrationMilestone(
        "M4", "Go-Live Approval", "Security review complete; production sign-off",
        owning_team="Security", target_date=date(2025, 9, 25),
        gates=["M3"],
    ),
]

# Check what is ready to start vs blocked
today = date(2025, 8, 15)
print("Ready to start:", [m.name for m in plan.ready_to_start(today)])
# → ["API Ready", "RAG Ready"]  (M3 gated on M1+M2, M4 gated on M3)

print("Blocked:", [m.name for m in plan.blocked_milestones(today)])
# → ["Integration Test", "Go-Live Approval"]

The weekly milestone readiness report is the program manager's primary communication to stakeholders. Claude generates this from live plan data so the narrative always reflects current gate status and slip calculations:

import anthropic

def generate_milestone_readiness_report(
    plan: IntegrationPlan,
    as_of: date,
) -> str:
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()

    lines = []
    for m in plan.milestones:
        ach = plan.achieved_ids()
        gate_status = "gates met" if m.is_ready_to_start(ach) else f"waiting on {m.gates}"
        slip = m.slip_days(as_of)
        lines.append(
            f"- [{m.milestone_id}] {m.name} | {m.owning_team} | "
            f"target {m.target_date} | status {m.status.value} | "
            f"slip {slip}d | {gate_status}"
        )

    message = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=400,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"""
You are a program manager writing a milestone readiness report for {plan.program}.
Date: {as_of}

Milestones:
{chr(10).join(lines)}

Write a concise readiness report (≤180 words):
1. Overall integration health: GREEN / AMBER / RED and why
2. Which milestones are at risk of delaying downstream work
3. What actions the program manager should take this week
"""}],
    )
    return message.content[0].text

The milestone sync is a focused 30-minute meeting that only happens when gates are relevant — not as a standing weekly call when nothing is blocked. Claude generates the agenda from the current plan state:

def generate_milestone_sync_agenda(
    plan: IntegrationPlan,
    as_of: date,
) -> str:
    """Generate a structured milestone sync meeting agenda."""
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()

    blocked = plan.blocked_milestones(as_of)
    ready   = plan.ready_to_start(as_of)

    blocked_txt = "
".join(
        f"  - {m.name} (gates: {', '.join(m.gates)}; owner: {m.owning_team})"
        for m in blocked
    )
    ready_txt = "
".join(
        f"  - {m.name} (owner: {m.owning_team}, target: {m.target_date})"
        for m in ready
    )

    message = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=350,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"""
Generate a 30-minute milestone sync meeting agenda for {plan.program}.

Milestones ready to start:
{ready_txt if ready_txt else "  None"}

Milestones blocked on gates:
{blocked_txt if blocked_txt else "  None"}

Structure the agenda with time allocations. Include:
- Standing items (status updates, no surprises)
- Gate review for blocked milestones
- Owner confirmations for ready milestones
- Decision items
- Action items capture

Format as a numbered list with times.
"""}],
    )
    return message.content[0].text
Program manager notifies consumer team lead within 24h
Gate not achieved by due date
Immediate escalation to director; consumer team sprint replanned
Risk flagged in program risk register; contingency evaluated
Plan updated; CPM re-run; affected teams notified same day
Assuming gates are met without confirmation
: Product teams start integration build assuming the API is ready, only to find it was provisioned but not security-approved. Milestone gates require explicit sign-off, not assumed completion.
: A milestone showing 'in progress' at 80% is not 'almost ready' — the remaining 20% may contain the hardest integration work. Gates should require a concrete, testable artifact.
: Holding the milestone review with only the owning team present means the consumer team learns of delays in the following sprint review, not in time to replan.
: If every sprint task is a milestone, the coordination overhead crushes velocity. Reserve integration milestones for true cross-team handoffs — typically 4–8 per quarter for a multi-team program.
A well-defined integration milestone answers all of the following before it is added to the plan:
What is the specific, testable artifact that marks this milestone achieved?
Which team owns delivery and who is the named accountable lead?
Which teams are unblocked by this milestone and what can they do once it is achieved?
What are the gate milestones that must be achieved first?
What is the minimum viable version of this milestone if the full version is late?

Marking a milestone achieved requires more than the owning team saying the work is done. The consumer team must confirm the artifact meets their requirements in their environment. This two-party acknowledgement prevents the "works on my machine" failure mode where a milestone is called complete but the consumer can't actually use it.

@dataclass
class MilestoneAchievement:
    milestone_id: str
    achieved_date: date
    provider_sign_off: str      # name / email of the provider team lead
    consumer_sign_off: str      # name / email of the consumer team lead
    evidence: str               # link to test run, PR, or artifact
    notes: str = ""

def record_achievement(
    plan: IntegrationPlan,
    achievement: MilestoneAchievement,
) -> None:
    """Update milestone status to ACHIEVED once both parties sign off."""
    milestone = next(
        (m for m in plan.milestones if m.milestone_id == achievement.milestone_id),
        None,
    )
    if not milestone:
        raise ValueError(f"Milestone {achievement.milestone_id} not found")

    if not achievement.provider_sign_off:
        raise ValueError("Provider sign-off required")
    if not achievement.consumer_sign_off:
        raise ValueError("Consumer sign-off required before marking achieved")

    milestone.status = MilestoneStatus.ACHIEVED
    milestone.actual_date = achievement.achieved_date
    print(
        f"Milestone {achievement.milestone_id} ({milestone.name}) achieved "
        f"on {achievement.achieved_date}. "
        f"Provider: {achievement.provider_sign_off} | "
        f"Consumer: {achievement.consumer_sign_off}"
    )

# Usage: M1 (API Ready) signed off by both parties
record_achievement(plan, MilestoneAchievement(
    milestone_id="M1",
    achieved_date=date(2025, 8, 28),
    provider_sign_off="platform-lead@company.com",
    consumer_sign_off="product-lead@company.com",
    evidence="https://ci.company.com/runs/4821",
))
# Now M3 (Integration Test) gates are partially met — only M2 still pending

Recording both provider and consumer sign-offs in the plan creates an audit trail that is useful during retrospectives when teams want to understand exactly when each gate opened and whether delays were in delivery or in consumer confirmation.

Sometimes a downstream team can start partial work when only some of their gates are met. Modeling this avoids the all-or-nothing trap where a team sits idle because one of three gate milestones is slightly late:
Milestones achieved on/before target ÷ total milestones
Mean slip days across all milestones this quarter
Days between provider completion and consumer sign-off
New gate dependencies discovered after PI planning ÷ total
Milestones added or removed after plan baseline ÷ total

Track these metrics quarterly and include them in the program retrospective. A rising hidden dependency rate typically signals that the demand intake process is not asking teams to list external dependencies completely during planning.

Integration milestone coordination gives multi-team programs a structured layer above individual sprint tracking. The data model captures gate dependencies explicitly, so the program manager knows at any moment which milestones are ready to start, which are blocked, and which downstream teams are at risk. Claude accelerates reporting and meeting design — generating readiness narratives and sync agendas from live plan data — so the program manager spends coordination time solving problems rather than aggregating status.
